channel.title: XCloak Blog
channel.description: Technical notes, releases, and detection engineering from XCloak.

item.title: Kafka is optional: designing for graceful degradation
item.description: Not every self-hosted deployment wants to run a Kafka cluster. Here's how XCloak treats it as an enhancement rather than a hard dependency, and where that tradeoff actually lives in the code.

item.title: Why every destructive SOAR action needs a human click
item.description: Playbooks can isolate a host or kill a process automatically — except they can't, not without someone approving it first. Here's the design behind that gate, and why it's not configurable.

item.title: Multi-tenancy and row-level security: what an internal audit found
item.description: A defense-in-depth layer we built but hadn't fully wired up — how we found it, why it mattered, and what changed.

item.title: Why we built our own Sigma engine
item.description: Sigma is a spec, not a library. Here's what we implemented, what we deliberately left out, and why field-level matching plus logsource pre-filtering was the right subset to start with.

item.title: How a log line becomes an alert
item.description: The full pipeline from agent telemetry to a dashboard notification — normalization, Sigma evaluation, correlation, and real-time delivery.

item.title: XCloak v0.2.0: Enterprise Security Hardening
item.description: v0.2.0 is the most significant XCloak release to date — httpOnly cookie auth, atomic rate limiting, PostgreSQL RLS now load-bearing, and Kafka wired end-to-end.

item.title: Building Enterprise Android MDM Without Google EMM
item.description: A Flutter foreground service that collects security telemetry and accepts management commands without requiring EMM enrollment or Device Owner mode.

item.title: How XCloak Maps Linux /proc/net to Process Names
item.description: What process owns this network connection? On Linux the answer lives in /proc — the inode bridge from socket table to PID to process name.

item.title: Closing the TOCTOU Gap in Redis Rate Limiting
item.description: Rate limiters look simple but contain a subtle concurrency trap. Here's how we found it and closed it with an atomic Lua script.
